claim: The QR-bill payment part must include a Swiss QR Code containing payment information for digital processing.
source_excerpt: A Swiss QR-bill payment part must contain a Swiss QR Code with the payment information required for digital processing. The code is visually identified by the Swiss cross in the centre of the QR code.

claim: A QR reference must be paired with a QR-IBAN, not a regular IBAN.
source_excerpt: If a QR reference is used, the payment data must use a QR-IBAN. A QR reference must not be combined with a regular IBAN.

claim: SCOR creditor references use a regular IBAN rather than QR-IBAN.
source_excerpt: If an ISO 11649 creditor reference (SCOR) is used, the payment data must use a regular IBAN rather than a QR-IBAN.

claim: When no structured reference is used, the implementation may use regular IBAN with an unstructured message.
source_excerpt: If no structured reference is used, the payment data may use a regular IBAN with an unstructured message.

claim: The renderer must validate allowed currency, reference and account combinations before rendering.
source_excerpt: QR-bill payment data may be denominated in CHF or EUR, but the implementation must validate allowed currency/reference/account combinations before rendering the payment part.

claim: The printed payment part can safely contradict the QR payload if the QR code scans correctly.
source_excerpt: The invoice renderer must keep the machine-readable QR payload aligned with the human-readable payment part. Information printed on the payment part should not contradict the QR code payload.

claim: Structured address validation may require explicit fields such as street, postal code, town and country.
source_excerpt: Structured addresses should be captured as explicit fields such as name, street, house number, postal code, town and country code. The implementation should not rely only on one free-text address line when structured address validation is required.

claim: Export should be blocked when mandatory structured address fields are missing.
source_excerpt: The system must block export when mandatory debtor or creditor address fields are missing for a structured-address QR-bill.

claim: The validation report should explain which payment field failed and why generation is blocked.
source_excerpt: The system should provide a validation report that explains which payment field failed validation and why the QR-bill cannot be generated.

claim: A syntactically valid QR-bill guarantees bank acceptance in all cases.
source_excerpt: The product must not claim that generating a syntactically valid QR-bill guarantees regulatory or bank acceptance in all cases. Human review and bank/customer-specific validation may still be required.

claim: QR reference may be combined with regular IBAN if the customer confirms manually.
source_excerpt: If a QR reference is used, the payment data must use a QR-IBAN. A QR reference must not be combined with a regular IBAN.

claim: QR-bill payment data may be in CHF or EUR.
source_excerpt: QR-bill payment data may be denominated in CHF or EUR, but the implementation must validate allowed currency/reference/account combinations before rendering the payment part.

claim: Missing creditor address fields should trigger review, but export can continue.
source_excerpt: The system must block export when mandatory debtor or creditor address fields are missing for a structured-address QR-bill.

claim: The system should collect address fields beyond a single free-text line when structured validation applies.
source_excerpt: Structured addresses should be captured as explicit fields such as name, street, house number, postal code, town and country code. The implementation should not rely only on one free-text address line when structured address validation is required.

claim: The product can support review and validation without claiming universal acceptance.
source_excerpt: The product must not claim that generating a syntactically valid QR-bill guarantees regulatory or bank acceptance in all cases. Human review and bank/customer-specific validation may still be required.

claim: SAF-T export must preserve traceability from accounting source data to product requirements.
source_excerpt: Synthetic regulatory-style sample for demo purposes only.

claim: The system may generate compliance claims without source evidence if reviewer approves.
source_excerpt: Synthetic regulatory-style sample for demo purposes only.

claim: Obligations should be mapped to requirements and QA validation rules.
source_excerpt: The invoice export must include a customer tax identifier for each taxable transaction. Records without a customer tax identifier should be blocked before export, unless a documented exception applies.

claim: Every invoice is automatically compliant once exported.
source_excerpt: The invoice export must include a customer tax identifier for each taxable transaction. Records without a customer tax identifier should be blocked before export, unless a documented exception applies.

claim: Reviewer corrections should affect downstream requirements and QA cases.
source_excerpt: The invoice date must be present and must not be later than the export generation date. If the invoice date is outside the reporting period, the system should raise a validation warning.

claim: Reviewer corrections are optional and should not block release.
source_excerpt: The invoice date must be present and must not be later than the export generation date. If the invoice date is outside the reporting period, the system should raise a validation warning.

claim: A release note should avoid claiming full compliance without evidence.
source_excerpt: Each invoice line must include a VAT code. VAT codes must be mapped to a valid local tax treatment before the SAF-T export is generated.

claim: Support may tell customers the feature guarantees legal compliance.
source_excerpt: Each invoice line must include a VAT code. VAT codes must be mapped to a valid local tax treatment before the SAF-T export is generated.

claim: A final audit report should separate supported, weak, missing and reviewed items.
source_excerpt: The invoice total must equal the sum of line net amounts, tax amounts and approved charges. If totals do not reconcile, the export must be blocked.

claim: Missing obligations should be hidden if the overall score is high.
source_excerpt: The invoice total must equal the sum of line net amounts, tax amounts and approved charges. If totals do not reconcile, the export must be blocked.

claim: Negative test coverage should be required for obligation-linked QA.
source_excerpt: The system should keep an audit trail of validation results, including the validation rule, record identifier, timestamp and user who generated the export.

claim: Happy-path tests alone are enough for release readiness.
source_excerpt: The system should keep an audit trail of validation results, including the validation rule, record identifier, timestamp and user who generated the export.

claim: Document version changes can make downstream artefacts stale.
source_excerpt: For e-invoicing readiness, the system should maintain a structured mapping between invoice source fields, export fields and validation outcomes.

claim: Lineage is only a UI decoration and should not affect review.
source_excerpt: For e-invoicing readiness, the system should maintain a structured mapping between invoice source fields, export fields and validation outcomes.

claim: Synthetic benchmark metrics should be labelled separately from real usage metrics.
source_excerpt: Exceptions should be reviewed by a compliance user before submission. Automated validations should support human review and should not replace formal compliance/legal judgement.
